Amplifier
Introduction Architecture Map Design Philosophy The Kernel Module System The Orchestrator Tools vs Hooks Sessions Bundles Foundation Bridge Agents & Recipes Complete Picture Appendix
Amplifier
Introduction

Amplifier is a framework for building AI agents.

That sentence is deliberately simple, because the idea it describes is not. An AI agent (a program that can reason about a task, take actions, observe the results, and decide what to do next) is a genuinely complex thing to build well. The model itself (Claude, GPT, Gemini, whatever comes next month) handles the reasoning. But everything around the model (what tools it can use, what it remembers, what rules it follows, how it talks to the outside world) all needs structure. Amplifier provides that structure.

The project describes itself as an agentic harness. A harness, in the traditional sense, takes raw power and gives it direction. A climbing harness lets you use gravity safely. An electrical harness routes power to the right places. An agentic harness does the same thing for an AI model: it wraps around the model and gives it a controlled way to interact with the world.

How it works, in one analogy

If you’ve used a computer, you’ve used an operating system. An operating system has a kernel at its center. The kernel is tiny relative to the whole system. It handles the most fundamental things: managing memory, scheduling tasks, letting programs talk to hardware. Everything else (the web browser, the file manager, the apps) runs on top of the kernel, plugging into it through well-defined interfaces.

Amplifier works the same way, but for AI agents instead of computer programs. At the center is a small, unchanging core: the kernel. It handles the fundamentals: loading modules, dispatching events, managing sessions. Everything else (which AI model to use, what tools the agent can access, how conversations are stored, what rules the agent follows) is a module that plugs in from the outside.

This means you can swap out any piece without touching the center. A new AI model comes out? Write a new provider module. Need the agent to follow safety rules? Add a hook module. The kernel doesn’t care. It provides the infrastructure; the modules provide the behavior.

What this document covers
  • Architecture map
  • Design philosophy
  • The kernel (5 components)
  • Module system (6 types)
  • Tools vs Hooks
  • Sessions & bundles
  • The complete picture
Architecture Map

The whole system, in one diagram.

Thirteen clusters, every major component, and the connections between them. Think of it as a city map before you walk the streets. You don’t need to know every building. You need the neighborhoods.

Rendering architecture diagram…

Explore full diagram

At the center is the kernel, the small core that everything else connects to. Inside it, you’ll see the Coordinator (a registry that stores modules), the Hook and Event system (a notification network), the Module Loader (how pieces get added), and the Session lifecycle (how a conversation starts and ends).

Around the kernel are the modules, the pluggable pieces that define how an agent actually behaves. The Orchestrator (which drives the conversation), Providers (which connect to AI models), Tools (actions the agent can take), Context Managers (which handle memory), and Hook Handlers (which enforce rules).

On the outer edge is the Foundation layer, which turns human-readable configuration files into running agents, and the ecosystem: bundles, recipes, apps, and community extensions.

Don’t try to memorize this. Use it as a reference. As we walk through each piece, this map will make more sense. By the time we reach the final section, every cluster on this diagram will be familiar.

Reading the diagram
  • Arrow direction = dependency direction
  • Cluster = a group of related components
  • Click “Explore full diagram” to zoom & pan
  • Click any node to highlight its connections
Design Philosophy

Three ideas shaped how Amplifier is built.

They weren’t written on a whiteboard first. They emerged from the code, from decisions made repeatedly about what belongs in the kernel and what doesn’t.

Mechanism, not policy

The kernel provides a hook system but never decides what should be blocked. A safety module decides. A logging module decides. The kernel provides the mechanism; modules provide the policy.

The center stays still

New AI models, new paradigms, new tooling: none of it touches the kernel. The center stays still so the edges can move at any speed. Stability at the center enables velocity at the edges.

Ruthless simplicity

Every line in the kernel is a future constraint. The kernel does zero file I/O. Not because persistence isn’t important, but because the kernel isn’t the right place for it.

Mechanism, not policy

The kernel has a hook system. Modules react when things happen. A tool is about to be called; the kernel fires an event. Registered hooks respond. A safety module might block the call. A logging module observes it. An approval module pauses and asks a human.

The kernel never decides which response is correct. It fires events and collects results. The decision belongs to modules. This separation comes from operating system design. The Linux kernel provides scheduling and memory management without deciding how programs use those mechanisms. Amplifier applies the same pattern everywhere.

The center stays still

New AI models come out every few months. Entirely new paradigms emerge: multi-modal models, reasoning models, models that can use computers directly. When a new model arrives, someone writes a new provider module, one that connects to that model’s API and translates between Amplifier’s internal format and the model’s format. The kernel doesn’t know or care whether it’s talking to Claude, GPT, Gemini, or a model that doesn’t exist yet.

Ruthless simplicity

The kernel does zero file I/O. It doesn’t write logs. It doesn’t save conversation transcripts. It doesn’t persist configuration. Every line of code in the kernel is a line that every module depends on. So the kernel is ruthless about what it includes. If something can be a module, it must be a module.

You can search the entire kernel codebase for file-writing operations and find zero matches. That’s not an accident. It’s the most concrete expression of this philosophy.
The Kernel

The kernel contains exactly five things. Everything else is outside it.

In any operating system, the kernel is the part that’s always running. Amplifier borrows this idea directly. The kernel (a package called amplifier-core) is the small, unchanging center of the system. It’s the piece that every module depends on.

1

Hook & Event System

When something happens (a message is sent, a tool is called, a session starts) the system fires an event. There are 41 of these events. Hooks are what respond. Events and hooks are the same system. Implemented in Rust for performance.

2

Module Loader

Discovers available modules, loads them, and gives each one a chance to set itself up. Discovery happens through Python entry points and filesystem scanning. Supports Python, WebAssembly, and remote (gRPC) modules.

3

Contracts (Protocols)

Six module types defined as Python Protocols using structural subtyping. If a class has the right methods, it satisfies the Protocol. No base class required. No explicit registration. Small, stable contracts that rarely change.

4

The Coordinator

A passive registry: a shared shelf where modules are stored. Three operations: mount, get, unmount. Despite the name, it doesn’t coordinate anything. The Orchestrator module does the actual coordinating. Rust struct via PyO3.

5

Session Lifecycle

A session is one conversation. Four phases: Create, Initialize, Execute, Cleanup. Fixed loading order. Orchestrator and Context Manager are required. Everything else is optional. Cleanup runs in a finally block. Always.

Key Fact

The kernel writes zero bytes to disk. No log files, no transcripts, no configuration files. Search the entire amplifier-core codebase for file writes: zero matches. Mechanism, not policy, made tangible.

The Rust and Python boundary

Amplifier is written in two languages, and this is a deliberate architectural choice. Python is the language users see. Module authors write Python. The API surface (the methods you call, the objects you create) is Python.

Rust is the language the kernel runs in. The Coordinator, the Hook Registry, the Cancellation Token, the Session object: all of these are Rust structs, compiled to native code, exposed to Python through a bridge library called PyO3. The Python files in amplifier-core are thin wrappers.

For module authors, this is invisible. You write Python. You don’t need to know Rust exists. But when you call coordinator.get("provider"), you’re reaching into a compiled Rust data structure through a Python wrapper. The performance characteristics reflect that.

Python API 
# Python wrapper (thin)
class Coordinator:
    # Rust struct under the hood
    def mount(self, name, module): ...
    def get(self, name): ...
    def unmount(self, name): ...

# Rust struct, via PyO3
# coordinator.rs line 47
Module System

Everything outside the kernel is a module.

A module is a self-contained piece that plugs in through a standard interface to add a specific capability. Six types, one universal contract, fixed loading order. Remove one, add another. The rest of the system doesn’t notice.

Orchestrator

The conductor

Drives the conversation. Decides what context the AI needs, which provider handles each request, how tool calls are handled, when to stop. Exactly one per session. Required.

Required · 1 per session
Provider

The AI connection

Connects to an AI model. Claude, GPT, Gemini, local model. Each connection is a separate Provider. Translates between Amplifier’s format and the model’s API. Multiple per session allowed.

Optional · multiple allowed
Tool

The agent’s hands

An action the agent can take. Read a file. Search the web. Run a command. The AI decides when to use tools. It sees them listed in its instructions. Visible, intentional, under the model’s control.

Optional · AI-visible
Context Manager

The agent’s memory

Keeps track of what’s been said and decides what to include when the AI needs context. Handles the token budget constraint and decides which messages fit in the AI’s limited context window. Exactly one per session. Required.

Required · 1 per session
Hook Handler

The sentinel

Reacts to events. Fires automatically when something happens. The AI has no idea hooks exist. They are invisible to the model, not listed in its instructions, never chosen by it. Observe, block, inject, or modify.

Optional · AI-invisible
Resolver

The path finder

Maps module identifiers (names like anthropic-provider) to actual locations on disk. Infrastructure plumbing. The Foundation layer mounts one. Users rarely write these.

Infrastructure

The universal mount contract

Every module type plugs in through the same function.

The contract is a function called mount. It receives two things: the Coordinator (the registry where all modules are stored) and a configuration dictionary. It does whatever setup it needs to do, registers itself into the Coordinator, and optionally returns a cleanup function that will be called when the session ends.

The Orchestrator follows this contract. The Provider follows this contract. Tools, Context Managers, Hook Handlers, and Resolvers all follow this contract. The Module Loader doesn’t need to know what type of module it’s loading. It calls mount, and the module takes care of the rest.

Loading order

Modules are loaded in a fixed order: Orchestrator first, then Context Manager, then Providers, then Tools, then Hook Handlers last. The Orchestrator and Context Manager are required. If either fails to mount, the session stops immediately with an error. The rest are optional.

Python 
# Universal mount contract
# Every module type uses this.
async def mount(
    coordinator,   # the registry
    config: dict   # configuration
) -> Optional[cleanup_fn]:
    # 1. Set up the module
    # 2. Mount into Coordinator
    # 3. Return cleanup (optional)
    coordinator.mount(
        "tool.my_tool", self
    )
    return self.cleanup
Loading order
Orchestrator required
Context Manager required
Providers optional
Tools optional
Hook Handlers optional
The Orchestrator

The Orchestrator isn’t just another module. It’s the only one with live access to the full system.

Every other module is isolated after mount. The Orchestrator gets the Coordinator at runtime, on every call. That privilege gap defines how the entire agent loop works.

 Most Privileged Module

The only module with live access to the full system

Every module in Amplifier receives the Coordinator (the registry where modules are stored) once: at mount time. The module uses it to register itself, and then the Coordinator reference is gone.

The Orchestrator is the exception. It receives the Coordinator again, at runtime, every time it’s called. This means the Orchestrator can, at any moment during execution, reach into the registry and access any module: the Context Manager, every Provider, every Tool, every Hook Handler.

No other module can do this. A Provider can’t look up which Tools are available. A Tool can’t check which Provider is being used. Only the Orchestrator has a live connection to the full system at runtime. This is by design, not by accident.

What happens when you send a message

1

You send a message

The session receives it and hands it to the Orchestrator.

2

Orchestrator fetches context

Calls the Context Manager to get conversation history (what’s been said, what tools have been used).

3

Events fire; hooks may inject context

Before the AI sees anything, hook handlers can inject additional context. “Remember: this user prefers concise answers.” The AI will read this as part of the natural conversation.

4

Provider calls the AI model

The Orchestrator calls a Provider. The Provider sends the assembled context to the AI model and waits for a response.

5

If the AI wants a tool, hooks check first

Before the tool executes, an event fires. Hook Handlers can observe, modify, or block the call. A safety hook might say “that tool call is trying to delete system files. Denied.” If no hook blocks the call, the tool executes.

6

After the tool runs, hooks inject feedback

A linter hook might run after a file-writing tool, find errors, and inject “The linter found 3 errors in lines 12, 15, and 22.” The AI reads this on its next iteration.

7

Final response returns as a string

When the AI is done, the Orchestrator returns a plain text string. All rich data (token usage, tool history) exits through hook events as a side channel.

The agent loop (the “think, act, observe, repeat” cycle) is not in the kernel. It lives in an Orchestrator module called loop-basic. A different Orchestrator module could implement a completely different strategy. The kernel wouldn’t change.
Tools vs Hooks

Tools are capabilities the AI controls. Hooks control the AI without it knowing.

Both are modules. Both plug in the same way. But they operate in fundamentally different realms, and using the wrong one for a task produces unreliable behavior.

 Tools

The AI decides

Available tools are listed in the AI’s system prompt. The model sees each tool’s name, description, and input schema. When the model decides it needs to take an action, it generates a tool call. The model looks at the user’s request, considers its available tools, and chooses intentionally.

Tool calls appear in the conversation history. The result appears in the conversation history. Everything is visible, traceable, and explainable.

Tools extend what the model can do.

 Hooks

Code decides

Hook Handlers respond to events in the system. When something happens, an event fires. Hook Handlers that have registered for that event run automatically. The AI model has no idea that hooks exist.

Hooks do not appear in the model’s instructions. They are not listed in the system prompt. The model cannot see them, cannot call them, cannot choose to use them. Hooks operate entirely in the system layer.

Hooks govern what the model is allowed to do.

The linter problem

You want every file the AI writes to be linted. Two approaches:

As a tool: You give the AI a run_linter tool and tell it in its instructions to always run the linter after writing a file. The problem? The AI might forget. It might decide the linter isn’t needed this time. It might get sidetracked by the user’s follow-up question. You’re relying on the model’s compliance with an instruction, which is probabilistic, not guaranteed.

As a hook: You register a Hook Handler for the tool:post event, which fires after any tool completes. When the event fires for a file-writing tool, the hook runs the linter. If it finds errors, it injects a message: “The linter found 3 errors on lines 12, 15, and 22.” The AI reads this on its next turn and fixes the issues. The linting happens every single time, regardless of what the model decides.

The hook doesn’t interrupt the AI. It doesn’t send a signal or raise a flag. It simply places information where the AI will naturally encounter it. The AI does what language models do: read text and respond to it.

The hook priority cascade

When multiple hooks respond to one event, their results are resolved in this order, from highest authority to lowest:

Deny
Highest priority. One deny blocks the entire operation. No further hooks execute. Fail-closed security posture. A single safety hook can override everything.
AskUser
Pauses execution for a human decision. First one wins. You can’t merge approval requests. For operations that aren’t clearly dangerous but aren’t clearly safe either.
InjectContext
Multiple hooks accumulate. All messages are merged and added to conversation history. A linter hook can inject “3 syntax errors” while a style hook simultaneously injects “naming conventions violated.” The AI sees both.
Modify
Transforms data flowing through the event. Multiple modify hooks chain. Each sees the output of the previous. For data transformation pipelines: redact sensitive info, reformat output.
Continue
The default. No-op. “I’ve seen this event and I have nothing to add.” If a hook doesn’t care about an event, it returns continue. The event passes through unmodified.

The event system

All hooks are triggered by events. Amplifier defines 41 canonical events, organized by namespace. Each event name follows a consistent pattern: namespace:action. Session events are session:start, session:end. Tool events are tool:pre (before execution), tool:post (after execution). Provider events are provider:request, provider:response.

Events and hooks are the same system. There is no separate “event bus” and “hook registry.” It’s one unified dispatch mechanism (HookRegistry.emit()) that fires an event and collects responses from registered hooks.

When a hook handler throws an error, the error is swallowed. The pipeline continues. The error is logged, but it doesn’t crash the system. The only hook action that stops an operation is an explicit deny. Every event also gets automatically stamped with the session ID, parent session ID, and a UTC timestamp.

41 event namespaces
session:start   session:end tool:pre        tool:post provider:request   provider:response prompt:prepare   context:injected orchestrator:start   agent:*
Auto-stamped on every event
  • session_id
  • parent_id (if child session)
  • UTC timestamp
Sessions

A session is a conversation.

One agent interaction from start to cleanup. The container where kernel infrastructure, modules, the Orchestrator, tools, and hooks all come together.

01

Create

Session created with configuration. Coordinator and Module Loader are set up. Nothing has been loaded yet. The system knows what it needs, but hasn’t started yet.

02
Required: Orchestrator + Context

Initialize

Module Loader loads modules in fixed order. If the Orchestrator or Context Manager fails to load, the session stops. Everything else is optional.

03

Execute

Session receives a message. Passes it to the Orchestrator. Gets a response back. This phase is repeatable. Each call is one turn of conversation. Between turns, all state is maintained.

04

Cleanup

session:end fires first (hooks get a final chance). Then Coordinator cleans up modules. Then Loader cleans up. In a finally block. Always runs, even after errors.

Parent and child sessions

Sometimes one agent needs to delegate work to another. A coding agent might delegate a research task to a research agent. Amplifier supports this through parent-child sessions.

The mechanism is straightforward: when creating a new session, you can set a parent_id that links it to an existing session. That’s it. The child session is a full, independent session with its own Coordinator, its own modules, its own configuration. It doesn’t inherit anything from the parent automatically. No shared modules, no shared memory, no shared configuration. It’s linked by ID only.

This is deliberately minimal. A parent-child relationship in Amplifier means “this session was started because of that session.” It’s a record of provenance, not a mechanism for sharing. If you want the child session to have the same configuration as the parent, you pass the same configuration when creating it. Nothing is automatic.

Cancellation

Cancellation is cooperative. The kernel can’t forcibly stop an Orchestrator in the middle of its work. Instead, it sets a flag with three states: no cancellation requested, graceful cancellation (finish what you’re doing), and immediate cancellation (stop as soon as possible). The Orchestrator must check this flag periodically. If it ignores the flag, cancellation doesn’t happen.

Two implementations

Sessions have two parallel implementations: AmplifierSession (Python) and RustSession (Rust via PyO3). At the package level, AmplifierSession is re-exported as RustSession.

On-disk files come from hooks
  • transcript.jsonl: logging hook
  • events.jsonl: logging hook
  • metadata.json: logging hook
  • Remove hooks: files disappear. Session still runs.
Bundles & Configuration

Most Amplifier users will never write a module. They’ll write a bundle.

A bundle is a text file (no code required) that describes which modules to load, what context to give the AI, and how the session should behave.

A bundle is a text file that describes which modules to load, what context to give the AI, and how the session should behave. If modules are the building blocks of an agent, a bundle is the blueprint. The simplest bundle is a single file. You can create an AI agent by writing a few lines of configuration and a paragraph of instructions.

Two formats

Markdown with YAML frontmatter is the more common format. The file has two parts: a block of YAML configuration at the top (between --- markers), and a Markdown body below it. The YAML says what modules to load and how to configure them. The Markdown body becomes the AI’s system instruction: its personality, its rules, its sense of purpose.

Pure YAML is configuration only, with no prose instruction body. Used when a bundle is providing configuration (loading certain tools, setting session parameters) but doesn’t need to tell the AI anything directly.

Composition

A bundle can declare an include: list of references to other bundles. When the system prepares the bundle, it loads the included bundles first, then applies the current bundle’s configuration on top. Last wins. Different parts of the configuration merge differently: session settings are deep-merged, module lists are merged by key, instruction text is replaced entirely (there’s no sensible way to merge two paragraphs of prose).

This composition system lets you build up configurations in layers. A base bundle might define a standard set of tools and safety hooks. A team bundle includes the base and adds team-specific tools. A project bundle includes the team bundle and overrides the AI model.

A minimal bundle
bundle.md 
---
bundle:
  model: anthropic/claude
  tools:
    - name: filesystem
    - name: web-search
  hooks:
    - name: safety-guard
---

You are a helpful coding assistant.
You write clean, well-documented code.
You follow the project’s coding
standards (see @project:standards.md).
Merge rules
  • Session settings: deep merge
  • Providers / Tools / Hooks: merge by key
  • Instruction text: replace entirely

The @mention system

Inside a bundle’s instruction body, you can reference external files using @namespace:path/to/file.md. When the system encounters an @mention, it resolves the reference to a file on disk, reads its content, and includes it in the system prompt that the AI receives.

This is how you give an agent background knowledge without cramming everything into one enormous instruction file. Your coding standards document, project overview, list of API endpoints. Each lives in its own file, and the bundle references whichever ones are relevant.

The resolution process is recursive, up to three levels deep. Content is deduplicated by SHA-256 content hash. If two different @mentions resolve to the same content through different paths, the content appears only once.

@mention syntax
@project:standards/coding.md
@project:docs/api-reference.md
@~/shared/company-guidelines.md
Live context files

@mention files are re-read on every single turn of conversation through the System Prompt Factory. Edit a context file while an agent is running, and the agent sees the change on its very next turn. No restart required.

Foundation Bridge

A bundle is a text file. The kernel expects structured data. Foundation translates.

Foundation (amplifier-foundation) reads bundle files, resolves references, downloads needed modules, and produces a configuration the kernel can work with. It bridges how people describe agents and how the system runs them, and stays connected at runtime.

PreparedBundle

Foundation’s output has four parts: a mount plan (which modules to load, in what slots, with what configuration), a resolver (maps module names to file paths), the original bundle (preserved for reference), and package paths (for cleanup at session end).

The eight-step pipeline

Compile mount plan → create virtualenv → install packages → collect module specs → download modules → persist install state → create resolver → package into PreparedBundle. The pipeline absorbs all practical mess: dependencies, downloads, path resolution. The kernel receives a clean mount plan and a resolver. Nothing else.

The kernel doesn’t need Foundation. The kernel works with a raw configuration dictionary and a set of module paths. Foundation is a user-experience layer. It makes the system approachable by translating text files into running sessions.
Eight-step pipeline
1Compile mount plan
2Create activator
3Install Python packages
4Collect module specs
5Download/install modules
6Persist install state
7Create resolver
8Return PreparedBundle

Two persistent callback channels

Most preparation layers do their work and disappear. Foundation does the preparation and then stays connected. It registers two callbacks that remain active for the session’s entire lifetime.

 System Prompt Factory

Called on every single turn

Every time the AI is about to think (every single turn of conversation) Foundation re-reads all @mention files, resolves nested @mentions up to three levels deep, deduplicates content by SHA-256 hash, and rebuilds the complete system prompt from the instruction text plus all resolved context.

This is why @mention files are live. The kernel calls a function and gets a string back. Foundation is the one doing the file I/O, all on every turn.

 BundleModuleResolver

Called on demand during loading

When the kernel’s Module Loader needs to find a module (to resolve a module identifier like tool-filesystem to an actual file path) it calls the resolver. That resolver was created by Foundation during the preparation pipeline.

Foundation code is running inside the kernel’s dispatch path, even though the kernel has no dependency on Foundation and no knowledge of how the resolution works.

Agents, Context Files & Recipes

Agents, context files, and recipes: all built from existing primitives.

Three concepts that sound like major features. None require new kernel support. Each is assembled from pieces already covered.

Agents are bundles

Agents are bundles. Same file format. Same parser. Same composition, @mentions, and merge rules.

One difference: the YAML frontmatter key. A bundle uses bundle:. An agent uses meta:. That’s it. Everything else (how the file is read, how instructions are parsed, how modules load) is identical.

The kernel has no concept of “agents.” It runs sessions with configurations. The naming is a semantic signal for humans: agent files represent complete autonomous entities, bundle files represent configuration fragments.

Context files

A context file is a Markdown document that gets loaded into the AI’s working memory at the start of each conversation turn. Context files are what @mentions reference. When a bundle’s instruction body says @project:standards/coding.md, it’s pointing to a context file. That file might describe the project’s coding conventions, its preferred frameworks, its naming patterns.

The value of context files is separation of concerns. Instead of cramming every piece of background knowledge into the bundle’s instruction body, you put each topic in its own file. One file for coding standards. One file for the API reference. One file for the team’s workflow. The bundle references whichever files are relevant. One file, one source of truth. Update it once, and every agent and every bundle that references it picks up the change.

Context files are also composable. A context file can contain @mentions of its own, referencing other context files, up to three levels deep.

Agent vs Bundle
agent file 
---
meta:            <-- agent
  name: coder
---
bundle file 
---
bundle:          <-- bundle
  name: tools
---

One key difference. Same parser. Same everything else.

Recipes: multi-step workflows

Sometimes a task is too complex for a single agent conversation. It involves multiple steps, each requiring different capabilities, where the output of one step feeds into the next. Recipes handle this.

Sequential execution

A recipe is a YAML file that defines a sequence of steps. Each step runs its own agent session with its own bundle, tools, and instructions. Output from step N becomes input to step N+1. Review code → Write docs → Create pull request.

Approval gates

Points in the sequence where execution pauses and waits for a human decision. Between steps, a person can review the current output before the system continues. Automated work with human judgment at the critical junctions.

Resumability

Each step creates a checkpoint. If something fails at step four of a seven-step recipe, you don’t need to start over from step one. Resume from the last successful checkpoint. No work lost to a network error.

Recipes are themselves a bundle concept, not a kernel concept. The kernel doesn’t know recipes exist. The recipe system is built on top of the kernel’s session API. It creates sessions, runs them, collects outputs, and chains them together.
The Complete Picture

Six layers, one ecosystem.

Each layer depends only on the one below. Replace anything at one layer without affecting the others. This isn’t conceptual; it’s the literal code dependency structure.

Layer 6: Community
Third-party modules, bundles, recipes, integrations. Open at every level.
Anyone can build here
Layer 5: Applications
CLI, web interface, IDE integration, voice assistant. The things people use.
User surfaces
Layer 4: Bundles
Configuration packages. Text files. Combine modules into deployable agents. No code required.
Configuration
Layer 3: Modules
Providers, tools, hooks, orchestrators, context managers, resolvers. The actual capabilities.
Pluggable behavior
Layer 2: Foundation
Turns bundles into running sessions. Finds modules, assembles config, and stays connected at runtime.
Optional bridge
Layer 1: Kernel
Contracts, module loading, event dispatch, session lifecycle. The unchanging center.
Never changes

The pattern across all six layers is the same: each layer depends only on the one below. This means you can replace anything at one layer without affecting the layers above or below it. A new Foundation implementation could be written without changing the kernel. A new application could be built without changing Foundation. A new module could be published without any of the existing modules knowing it exists.

Where things belong

New AI model
Provider module → Layer 3
New safety rule
Hook module → Layer 3
Shared agent config
Bundle → Layer 4
New user interface
Application → Layer 5

The architecture diagram, fully explained

Click to open the interactive architecture diagram

Zoom, pan, and click any node to highlight its connections

“The kernel provides mechanism. Modules provide policy. The center stays still so the edges can move at any speed.”

The most interesting parts of the system are the ones that haven’t been built yet.

Appendix

Research Methodology

The facts in this document were established through a structured investigation of the Amplifier source code.

The approach

The investigation used a method called Parallax Discovery, a multi-agent, multi-pass approach to understanding a complex codebase. Rather than having one investigator trace through the code once, multiple independent investigators examine the same codebase from different angles, and their findings are reconciled afterward.

This approach surfaces assumptions. When two independent investigators arrive at the same conclusion through different paths, confidence in that conclusion is high. When they disagree, the disagreement itself is informative. It points to areas where the code is ambiguous.

How it worked

Four teams of investigators (seven agents in total) each examined different aspects of the Amplifier codebase. The teams worked independently. Each team produced findings with file-and-line-number evidence. Not “the Coordinator is a Rust struct” as a general claim, but “the Coordinator is defined as a Rust struct in amplifier-core/src/coordinator.rs at line 47.”

Investigation output
Verified claims 37
Independent investigators 7
Identified discrepancies 4
Consolidated unknowns 8
Reconciliation document 805 lines
Source packages
  • amplifier-core: Python + Rust (PyO3)
  • amplifier-foundation: Python
1 / 1

No speaker notes.

Amplifier Architecture Map

Introduction

Amplifier

A framework for building AI agents with predictable structure.

Introduction

What does an agent need?

The Model
handles the reasoning
  • Decides what to do next
  • Generates responses
  • Interprets tool results
The Harness
handles everything else
  • What tools can it use?
  • What does it remember?
  • What rules does it follow?
Introduction

The operating system analogy

An OS kernel
the unchanging center
  • Tiny relative to the whole
  • Everything plugs in through interfaces
  • Swap apps without touching it
Amplifier kernel
same structure, for AI agents
  • Small, unchanging core
  • Modules plug in around it
  • Swap any piece, center stays still
Introduction

What we will cover

Architecture map
The whole system at a glance
Design philosophy
Why it is shaped this way
The kernel
Five components, nothing more
Modules, tools, hooks
How capability is added
The complete picture
Six layers, one ecosystem
The Architecture of Amplifier
13 clusters · scroll to zoom · click any node to highlight connections
Architecture Map

Three concentric rings

The kernel (center)
Small, unchanging. Coordinator, events, loading, sessions.
Modules (middle ring)
Pluggable behavior: orchestrator, providers, tools, context, hooks.
Ecosystem (outer ring)
Foundation, bundles, apps, community extensions.
Design Philosophy

Three ideas shaped the architecture

Mechanism, not policy
The kernel provides capabilities. Modules decide how to use them.
The center stays still
New models, new paradigms: none of it touches the kernel.
Ruthless simplicity
Every line in the kernel is a future constraint. Keep it small.
Design Philosophy
“Mechanism, not policy.”
The kernel provides the hook system. It never decides what gets blocked.
Design Philosophy

The hook example

1
A tool is about to be called
2
The kernel fires a tool:pre event
kernel
3
Registered Hook Handlers run
mechanism
4
Safety hook blocks it. Logger observes it. Approval hook pauses it.
policy
5
Kernel collects results. Done.
kernel
Design Philosophy
The center stays still so the edges can move at any speed.
New AI model? Write a provider module. The kernel does not change.
Design Philosophy
0
bytes written to disk by the kernel
not logs, not transcripts, not config
Design Philosophy
The kernel is boring.
That boringness is the point.
Mechanism-not-policy + still center + ruthless simplicity = a core that never needs to change.
The Kernel
5
things inside the kernel
amplifier-core. Everything else is a module.
The Kernel

The five kernel components

Hook & Event System
41 events · unified dispatch · implemented in Rust
Module Loader
Discovers and mounts all modules at session start
Contracts (Protocols)
6 module types · structural subtyping · rarely change
The Coordinator
A registry. A labeled shelf for mounted modules.
Session Lifecycle
Create → Initialize → Execute → Cleanup
The Kernel

The Hook & Event system

1
Something happens (tool called, message sent, session starts)
2
Kernel fires a named event: namespace:action
Rust
3
Registered hooks for that event are called
dispatch
4
Hook results resolved by priority cascade
cascade
5
System continues (or halts if deny returned)
The Kernel

The Rust / Python boundary

Python
what you see
  • Module authors write Python
  • API calls use Python objects
  • Configuration is Python dicts
Rust
what runs underneath
  • Coordinator, HookRegistry, Sessions
  • Compiled to native code via PyO3
  • Hot paths run at compiled speed
The Kernel
Despite its name, the Coordinator does not coordinate. It is a registry.
mount() · get() · unmount(). Three operations, nothing more
The Kernel

Session lifecycle

Orchestrator + Context Manager are required. Others are optional. Cleanup runs even after errors.

1
Create
config in
→
2
Initialize
modules mount
→
3
Execute
repeatable
→
4
Cleanup
always runs
The Kernel

The universal mount contract

async def mount(
    coordinator: Coordinator,
    config: dict
) -> Optional[cleanup_fn]:
    # receive the registry + configuration
    # do your setup work
    # register yourself in the coordinator
    # optionally return a cleanup function

All six module types follow this exact interface.

The Kernel
Zero file writes.
Search the kernel codebase. Zero matches.
Logging = hook module. Persistence = context manager. Config = Foundation.
Module System
Everything outside the kernel
is a module.
Six types. One mount contract. Fixed loading order. Swap any piece through a standard interface.
Module System

Six module types

Orchestrator
Drives the conversation · exactly one per session · required
Provider
Connects to an AI model · Claude, GPT, Gemini, local
Tool
An action the agent can take · the AI decides when to use it
Context Manager
The agent's memory · manages the context window · required
Hook Handler
Reacts to events · invisible to the AI · any number
Resolver
Maps module names to file paths · infrastructure
Module System

Required vs Optional

Required
session fails without these
  • Orchestrator: no agent loop without it
  • Context Manager: no memory without it
  • Both must mount successfully
Optional
warn and continue if missing
  • Providers: you may have others
  • Tools: agent just has fewer actions
  • Hook Handlers: no observers
Module System

One contract for all six types

async def mount(
    coordinator: Coordinator,
    config: dict
) -> Optional[cleanup_fn]:
    ...

The Orchestrator follows this. The Provider follows this. All six module types, one interface.

Module System

Loading order is fixed

Each module can count on the ones loaded before it being available.

1
Orchestrator
required
→
2
Context
required
→
3
Providers
optional
→
4
Tools
optional
→
5
Hooks
optional
Module System

How modules are found

Python entry points
Install with pip. Module advertises itself automatically.
Filesystem scan
Directories named amplifier-module-* in search paths
WebAssembly
Sandboxed .wasm modules for isolated execution
Remote gRPC
Modules running on separate machines over the network
Module System
If you have the right methods, you qualify.
No base class required. No explicit registration. Structural subtyping via Python Protocols.
Module System

A preview: Tools vs Hooks

Tools
visible to the AI
  • Listed in the system prompt
  • The AI decides when to call them
  • Part of the model's deliberate reasoning
Hook Handlers
invisible to the AI
  • Not in the system prompt
  • Fire automatically on events
  • The model does not know they exist
The Orchestrator

The privilege gap

Every other module
receives Coordinator once
  • At mount time only
  • Does setup and registers
  • No live registry access after mount
The Orchestrator
receives Coordinator every turn
  • At mount time AND at runtime
  • Live access to every module
  • The only integration point
The Orchestrator

One turn of conversation

1
You send a message → Session passes it to the Orchestrator
2
Orchestrator asks Context Manager for conversation history
3
Events fire → hooks can inject context before the AI sees anything
4
Orchestrator calls Provider → AI model thinks
5
AI wants a tool? → hooks check (allow/block) → tool runs
hook checks
6
After tool: hooks inject feedback (linter errors, validation)
hook injects
7
Done? → Orchestrator returns a plain string response
The Orchestrator
Hooks intercept at two points:
before a tool runs, and after.
Before: observe, block, or modify. After: inject feedback. The AI reads injected context as if it were part of the natural conversation.
The Orchestrator
The Orchestrator returns a plain string.
Rich data exits through hook events.
Token usage, tool call history, thinking blocks: all observable through hooks, not the return value.
The Orchestrator
The agent loop is a module, not the kernel.
The default loop-basic module implements think → act → observe → repeat. Different Orchestrator = different agent strategy.
Tools vs Hooks

Tools extend. Hooks govern.

Tools
the AI decides
  • Listed in the system prompt
  • Model chooses when to call
  • Visible, intentional, traceable
  • Extend what the model can do
Hooks
code decides
  • Not in the system prompt
  • Fire automatically on events
  • Invisible to the AI
  • Govern what the model may do
Tools vs Hooks
Tools are listed in the system prompt.
The AI sees them. The AI chooses them.
Every tool call is visible in the conversation history. Intentional. Traceable. Explainable.
Tools vs Hooks
The AI has no concept that hooks exist.
They are not in its instructions.
Hooks fire on system events. They observe, block, modify, or inject context, all without the model's awareness.
Tools vs Hooks

The linter problem

As a tool
probabilistic compliance
  • Tell the AI: always run the linter
  • The AI might forget
  • The AI might decide not needed
  • Reliability depends on the model
As a hook
guaranteed execution
  • Register for tool:post on file writes
  • Linter runs automatically every time
  • AI reads errors on its next turn
  • Reliability is structural, not probabilistic
Tools vs Hooks

How inject_context works

1
Event fires. For example, a file-writing tool just completed
2
Hook Handler runs, does its work (linter, validator, etc.)
3
Hook returns inject_context with a message
4
Coordinator calls Context Manager: add_message()
5
On AI's next turn, the message is in the conversation history
6
AI reads it and responds, never knowing a hook put it there
Tools vs Hooks

The hook priority cascade

Deny
Operation blocked immediately. All remaining hooks stop. One deny overrides everything.
fail-closed
AskUser
Execution pauses. A human makes a decision. First request wins.
human-in-loop
InjectContext
Multiple hooks accumulate. All messages are merged. They all get through.
additive
Modify
Data transforms in chain. Each hook sees the previous hook's output.
pipeline
Continue
Default. I have seen this event. Nothing to add.
no-op
Tools vs Hooks
41
canonical events in the system
session:start · tool:pre · tool:post · provider:request · provider:response · ...
Tools vs Hooks
Hook errors are non-fatal.
Only an explicit deny stops an operation.
A crashed logging hook does not stop the tool call. Every event is auto-stamped: session ID, parent ID, UTC timestamp.
Sessions

Anatomy of a session

One session = one conversation. All modules, all state, one lifetime.

1
Create
config in
→
2
Initialize
modules mount
→
3
Execute
repeatable
→
4
Cleanup
always runs
Sessions

Inside each phase

Create
Configuration in. Coordinator and Loader set up. Nothing loaded yet.
Initialize
Fixed loading order. Orchestrator + Context required. Others optional.
Execute
User sends a message. Orchestrator runs the loop. Response comes back. Repeatable.
Cleanup
session:end fires. Modules clean up. Loader cleans up. Always runs (finally block).
Sessions

Parent-child sessions

What you might expect
automatic inheritance
  • Child inherits parent's context
  • Shared modules, shared memory
  • Changes in parent affect child
What Amplifier does
explicit and independent
  • Set parent_id. That is the only link.
  • No shared modules, memory, or config
  • Each session fully self-contained
Sessions
The kernel writes nothing.
Session files come from hook modules.
transcript.jsonl · events.jsonl · metadata.json: all written by hooks, never by the kernel.
Bundles
Most Amplifier users will never write a module. They'll write a bundle.
A text file. No code required. Describes which modules to load, what context to give the AI, how the session should behave.
Bundles

Two bundle formats

Markdown + YAML
bundle.md (the common format)
  • YAML frontmatter: modules and config
  • Markdown body: the AI's instructions
  • Natural for describing behavior in prose
  • System checks for this first
Pure YAML
bundle.yaml (config only)
  • No instruction body
  • For configuration fragments
  • Base layers, shared tool sets
  • When the bundle has no instructions
Bundles

A minimal bundle

---
# bundle.md
bundle:
  providers:
    - provider-anthropic
  tools:
    - tool-filesystem
    - tool-terminal

---

You are a helpful coding assistant.
You follow these project standards:
@project:standards/coding.md

The YAML handles technical configuration. The Markdown handles human communication. This is everything you need for a working agent.

Bundles

Bundle composition

1
Base bundle: standard tools + safety hooks
organization
2
Team bundle includes base, adds team-specific tools
team
3
Project bundle includes team, overrides model or instructions
project
4
Last definition wins. Each layer specifies only what it changes.
Bundles

Merge strategies

Session settings
Deep-merge. Change only what you need. Others inherit from base.
Module lists
Merge by key. Same name replaces. Other modules from base are preserved.
Instructions (Markdown body)
Full replacement. Merged prose would be incoherent.
Bundles

The @mention system

# In your bundle's instruction body:

You are a coding assistant. Follow:
@project:standards/coding.md
@project:architecture/overview.md

# Resolved to file contents.
# Recursive up to 3 levels.
# Deduplicated by SHA-256 hash.

Referenced files become part of the AI's system prompt on every turn.

Bundles
Context files are live documents,
not snapshots.
Re-read on every turn. Edit a file while the agent is running, the agent sees the change on its next turn. No restart required.
Foundation
Foundation translates text files
into running sessions.
amplifier-foundation, the bridge between bundles and the kernel.
Foundation

PreparedBundle: Foundation's output

Mount plan
Instructions for the kernel: which modules, which slots, which config
Resolver
Maps module names to actual file paths on disk
Bundle
The original configuration preserved for reference
Package paths
What was installed during preparation, for cleanup
Foundation

The preparation pipeline

Foundation handles all practical messiness. The kernel receives a clean mount plan.

1
Compile mount plan
→
2
Create virtualenv
→
3
Install packages
→
4
Collect specs
→
5
Download modules
→
6
Persist state
→
7
Create resolver
→
8
Return PreparedBundle
Foundation
The kernel has no knowledge
that Foundation exists.
Call the kernel session API directly. Foundation is a user-experience layer, not a requirement.
Foundation
Foundation does not just set up.
It stays connected.
Two persistent callbacks registered at session start, active for the entire session lifetime.
Foundation

Callback 1: The System Prompt Factory

1
The AI is about to think, every single turn
2
Kernel calls the System Prompt Factory callback (provided by Foundation)
callback
3
Foundation re-reads every @mention file fresh from disk
Foundation
4
Resolves nested @mentions (up to 3 levels), deduplicates by SHA-256
Foundation
5
Returns the complete system prompt as a string
6
The kernel sees a string. It does not know files were involved.
Foundation

Callback 2: The BundleModuleResolver

1
The kernel's Module Loader needs to find a module
2
Kernel calls the resolver function (provided by Foundation)
callback
3
Foundation code runs inside the kernel's dispatch path
Foundation
4
Returns the file path for that module
resolution
5
The kernel has no code dependency on Foundation
6
Connected at runtime through well-defined interfaces, not at compile time
Foundation

The runtime relationship

Foundation depends on kernel
in code
  • Calls kernel session API
  • Uses kernel contracts
  • Knows the kernel exists
Kernel calls Foundation
at runtime only
  • Calls registered callback functions
  • Does not know who provided them
  • Does not know Foundation exists
Agents & Recipes

Agents are bundles

Bundle file
bundle.md or bundle.yaml
  • Uses bundle: key in frontmatter
  • Configuration fragment
  • "These are the database tools"
Agent file
agent.md or agent.yaml
  • Uses meta: key in frontmatter
  • Complete autonomous entity
  • "This is a coding assistant"
Agents & Recipes
One context file. Referenced by many bundles.
Update once. Every agent picks up the change.
No restarts. No redeployment. The System Prompt Factory re-reads files on every turn.
Agents & Recipes

Context files in practice

Coding standards
Every coding agent references the same file. Update once, all agents update.
API reference
Keep the current schema. Agents always work with the latest version.
Project overview
Can reference sub-documents. Agent gets the full context tree.
Compliance rules
One source of truth for rules. Never stale.
Agents & Recipes

Recipes: multi-step workflows

Each step runs its own session with its own tools and instructions. Output from step N becomes input to step N+1.

1
Review code
analysis agent
→
2
Write docs
writing agent
→
3
Create PR
git agent
Agents & Recipes

What makes recipes practical

Approval gates
human-in-the-loop
  • Execution pauses at declared points
  • Human reviews before continuing
  • Human rejects → workflow stops
Resumability
checkpoint after each step
  • Failure at step 6 of 10?
  • Resume from step 5, not step 1
  • No need to re-run completed work
Agents & Recipes
Agents are bundles.
Recipes are sessions chained together.
Same kernel API throughout.
Complex behavior built from simple kernel mechanisms, not added to the kernel.
Complete Picture

Six layers, the full ecosystem

Layer 1
Kernel
Contracts, loading, events, lifecycle. The unchanging center. amplifier-core.
Layer 2
Foundation
Turns bundles into sessions. Two persistent callbacks. amplifier-foundation.
Layer 3
Modules
Providers, tools, hooks, orchestrators, context managers, resolvers.
Layer 4
Bundles
Text file configuration. No code required. Compose modules into agents.
Layer 5
Applications
CLI, web interface, IDE integration, voice. The things people use.
Layer 6
Community
Third-party modules, shared bundles, recipes. Open at every layer.
Complete Picture

Infrastructure layers (1–3)

Layer 1: Kernel
Contracts, loading, events, lifecycle. Never changes.
Layer 2: Foundation
Turns bundles into sessions. Stays connected at runtime via callbacks.
Layer 3: Modules
The building blocks. Providers, tools, hooks, orchestrators, context managers.
Complete Picture

Ecosystem layers (4–6)

Layer 4: Bundles
Text file configuration. No code required. Compose modules into deployable agents.
Layer 5: Applications
CLI, web, IDE, voice. The things people actually use.
Layer 6: Community
Third-party everything. Open at every level. Anyone can build here.
The Complete Architecture
Every cluster maps to a concept we covered · every arrow a dependency we traced
Complete Picture

The architecture tells you where things belong

New AI model?
→ Layer 3
  • Write a Provider module
  • Same interface as all providers
  • Kernel does not know the new model exists
New safety rule?
→ Layer 3
  • Write a Hook module
  • Register for tool:pre events
  • The AI never knows the rule exists
Complete Picture
The kernel provides mechanism.
Modules provide policy.
The center stays still so the edges can move at any speed.
The most interesting parts of the system are the ones that have not been built yet.
Appendix

How these facts were established

7 independent investigators
4 teams examining different aspects of the codebase
37 verified claims
Each confirmed by 2+ investigators with file-and-line evidence
4 identified discrepancies
Documented with conflicting evidence and impact assessment
8 consolidated unknowns
Documented for future investigation
805-line reconciliation doc
The sole source of facts for Sections 1 through 12
